W2 or 1099? I used to think about this all the time when I first started my business, but the majority of my people to date are all 1099. I keep some people internally who are actually with me a majority of the time on W2, such as my project manager or such as my COO during certain instances, but even now I've converted a majority of my staff, I think besides one individual, to 1099 and I still have that other person at W2 that I plan on transitioning to 1099. When you have 1099 subcontractors, it's a little different than having W2 employees. First of all, you're not responsible for benefits when you have subcontractors, so you immediately take that expense off your pay. You also have less taxes that you're obligated to pay out to the government when you don't have as many W2 employees. However, when you have 1099 contractors, you have to be careful about how you call them and how you actually structure the naming system of different people in your organization. You see, I can't call these people employees, I can call them staff members. So I have 19 staff members inside of my business and they're 35% of them are my any, 65% over in Las Vegas and I have some scattered throughout the United States. That being said, everybody's on 1099 in my company because as subcontractors and not being in a physical environment where I'm constantly looking over them, I have obligation to lead them and give them resources and tools that could derive them success, but they're obligated for self-accountability, self-sustainability to generate the results that they have and I can fire them much easier just in case they're not generating results. So subcontractors can be held to different standards and different levels of accountability comparatively to what a W2 employee can. W2 employees after they're brought in are meant for the long run. They do give you some benefits because it looks as the government and you're creating a bunch of jobs comparatively, hiring a bunch of subcontractors and still moving money throughout the economy, so you get a little bit more benefits as an entrepreneur with W2. However, until you have a massive organization and you keep them all in-house inside of one physical location, I would not recommend that you go W2. I would tell you to stick with 1099, however, I'm not going to go too in depth on this video because I'm not a tax consultant and I don't want to put you in a position where you take all of my advice and not somebody who's a professional. I just want you to understand that in my business, from my experience, what's the name where I personally have done is swapped over to 1099 from the advising council that I've gotten and tax professionals that I hire inside of my organization. I've gotten this advice from people because I get tax breaks because of the ability that I have to fire people if necessary and from the ability for me to expect them to be accountable and I can treat them a little different than what I can if they were W2 employee. That's my advice to you. Actually put below a few different resources and people that are from my personal network that I have reached out to and said, hey, put your number in my TAP. If anybody calls or sends you an email or something, I just want you to know that that might happen. So if the person I have a lover, you guys are tax professional, both for business and for personal, feel free to reach out and ask them any questions that you have in relativity to this. Be sure if you guys like them enough to make sure you set up some kind of transaction with them where you guys give them some money or something to handle your business or do your accounting once a year. Do something with the people to make them feel good if you're just going to ask them a bunch of questions. That'd be my advice to you that way you get a valuable relationship out of the connections that I gave you. This has been the W2 versus 1099 course inside a team. I'm sorry, it's out of phase number seven, the team building phase. I'll see you in the next video.
